header: 🧭 Planning: where the AI-agent field goes next
description: Five concrete next-step research directions that recur across the Discussion / Limitations / Future Work sections of the 2023–2025 agent classics. Each step is grounded in passages where the authors themselves call out unresolved problems.

next_steps.title: Persistent autonomous agents
next_steps.subtitle: From single tasks to ongoing personas with stable memory
next_steps.summary: Move beyond episode-bounded agents that reset after each task toward persistent autonomous agents whose memory, goals, and learnt skills survive across days, weeks, and months — and that can be safely audited and rolled back.
next_steps.why_now: Reflexion, MemGPT, and Generative Agents show that long-horizon memory + skill libraries are feasible. The remaining gap is operational: persistence in the wild, secure state stores, and rollback / audit infrastructure. The first vendor that ships this primitive cleanly wins the agent-platform space.

next_steps.title: Self-improving agents
next_steps.subtitle: Agents that edit their own prompts, code, and architectures
next_steps.summary: Push past prompt-engineering-as-a-static-art toward agents that revise their own system prompts, propose new tools, and even modify their own scaffolding code based on observed task outcomes — a closed loop between agent behaviour and agent design.
next_steps.why_now: Self-Refine and Self-Discover have shown one-step self-improvement on single tasks. Extending this to multi-task, multi-deployment, durable improvement is the obvious next research move, but raises immediate safety questions that need solving in parallel.

sentences.text: Our results suggest that the strong performance in answering medical questions may be an emergent ability28 of LLMs combined with effective instruction prompt tuning.

next_steps.title: Verifiable and safe agents
next_steps.subtitle: Formal guarantees, human-in-the-loop, oversight
next_steps.summary: Develop formal verification, human-in-the-loop checkpoints, sandboxed execution, and provenance trails so that agentic systems acting on real-world resources can be reasoned about and trusted by the people deploying them — especially at organisational scale.
next_steps.why_now: Every agent paper from 2024 onwards (AutoGen, MetaGPT, SWE-agent, ChemCrow, Coscientist) flags safety, drift, and oversight as the blocker to production adoption. The research community now needs to move from anecdote to mechanism.

next_steps.title: Multimodal embodied agents
next_steps.subtitle: Vision-Language-Action models in the real world
next_steps.summary: Bring the agentic reasoning advances from text-only domains into multimodal Vision-Language-Action models that perceive screens, cameras, robot arms, and instruments — and act on them under the same planning + memory + tool-use loop.
next_steps.why_now: PaLM-E, RT-2, Computer Use, and the Mind2Web / WebArena lineage have demonstrated that the modality bridge is workable. The agent community's open question is unifying these with the discrete reasoning machinery of CoT / ToT / Reflexion.

sentences.text: Intended Uses VisualWebArena is a research benchmark to measure and evaluate the progress of multimodal agents.
It is primarily meant to act as a self-contained sandbox environment for safely building robust agents.
The models we presented in this paper are research prototypes, and not intended for deployment in practical applications in their current state (especially in high risk domains).
VisualWebArena represents a significant step to- wards addressing the gap in the evaluation of multi- modal agents on visually grounded tasks.
We proposed to build an embodied language model by in- jecting multi-modal information such as images into the em- bedding space of a pre-trained LLM.

Experiments showed that off-the-shelf state-of-the-art vision-language models trained on general VQA and captioning tasks are not sufﬁ- cient for embodied reasoning tasks, as well as limitations of a recent proposal for grounding language models through affordances.

To overcome these limitations, we proposed PaLM-E, a single model that is able to control different robots in simulation and in the real world, while at the same time being quantitatively competent at general VQA and captioning tasks.
PaLM-E is trained on a mixture of diverse tasks across mul- tiple robot embodiments as well as general vision-language tasks.

next_steps.title: Scientific discovery agents
next_steps.subtitle: Closing the loop from hypothesis to wet-lab
next_steps.summary: Build research co-pilots that go beyond literature synthesis to plan, execute, and iterate genuine scientific experiments — coupling reasoning agents to laboratory automation, simulation pipelines, and experimental design loops with measurable speed-up against human-only baselines.
next_steps.why_now: ChemCrow, Coscientist, Biomni, and the agentic rare-disease system (Nature 2026) demonstrated that the components exist. The next step is end-to-end deployment with rigorous measurement of discovery rate against control.

sentences.text: We show that GNNs trained on a large and diverse set of first-principles calculations can enable the efficient discovery of inorganic materials, increasing the number of stable crystals by more than an order of mag- nitude.

Our findings raise interesting questions about the capabilities of deep-learning systems in the natural sciences: the application of machine-learning methods for scientific discovery has traditionally suffered from the fundamental challenge that learning algorithms work under the assumption of identically distributed data at train and test times, but discovery is inherently an out-of-distribution effort.
This includes discovery in unseen chemical spaces (for example, with more than four different elements), as well as on new downstream tasks (for example, predicting kinetic properties).

We introduced an open-source library, AutoGen, that incorporates the paradigms of conversable agents and conversation programming.

It features a unified conversation interface among the agents, along with an auto-reply mechanisms, which help establish an agent-interaction interface that capitalizes on the strengths of chat-optimized LLMs with broad capabilities while accommodating a wide range of applications.
Creating such agents is crucial to ensuring that a multi-agent workflow can effectively troubleshoot and make progress on a task.

We believe that more systematic work will be required to develop guidelines for application-specific agents, to create a large OSS knowledge base of agents, and to create agents that can discover and upgrade their skills (Cai et al., 2023).
Some instructions can have ambiguities, and the designer should either reduce them for preciseness or intentionally keep them for flexibility and address the different situations in other agents.
